title.zh: 第八回 西行問鬼 忘川授路
title.en: Chapter 8: Travelling West to Question the Dead; the River of Forgetting Grants a Road

zh: 西行人獨踏荒草
忘川水深問無聲
en: Westward the lone man treads the withered grass
The River of Forgetting runs deep; his questions find no voice

passages.zh: 卻說吳烈自璟同歿後，沉默如一口廢井，深而無響。朝中諸事，悉委昭明主持；軍務諸令，皆令副將代行。世人見之，或以為其傷痛已過，殊不知靜水之下，萬壑俱在。某夜，烈獨立城頭，望西天雲色如鐵，忽心中有一念倏起：璟同在何處？非詩人之問，乃武者之問——刀問向西，腳步便西去。翌日天明，城門尚閉，守卒見一人獨行，背負短刀，步履如常，面無表情，以為流放之犯或遊蕩之鬼，皆閃道旁，不敢攔問。吳烈遂西行，無人相送，無旗無號，如石投入水，世界悄然合攏其後。
passages.en: After Jing Tong died, Wu Lie fell silent as an abandoned well — deep and without echo. The affairs of court he left entirely to Zhao Ming; military orders he delegated to his lieutenants. Those who observed him thought perhaps his grief had passed, not knowing that beneath still water a thousand gorges persist. One night Wu Lie stood alone on the city wall, watching the western sky where clouds massed dark as iron. A thought rose in him without warning: where is Jing Tong? Not a poet's question — a warrior's question. His blade turned west; his feet followed. At the next dawn, while the city gates were still shut, the gate-guards saw a lone man departing, a short sword at his back, his stride unhurried, his face empty of expression. They took him for a convict sent into exile, or a wandering ghost. They stepped aside and did not dare stop him or ask. Wu Lie went west. No one saw him off; no banner, no drum. Like a stone dropped into water, the world closed quietly behind him.

passages.zh: 行數日，地形漸異。道路初似尋常荒野，繼而草色轉白，繼而草中有聲，低如囈語，細聽之，乃人語，然語序顛倒，詞義散亂，如被水浸泡過久之書帛，字跡猶在而意思已失。吳烈蹲身，俯耳於草，辨出一句：「……娘……鍋裡……還有……」，又一句：「……別，別走……」，又一句支離不可辨認。他明白了——此乃死者臨終之言，散落此地，在草莖間反覆呢喃，如磁片碎於地，仍依稀記得舊日之歌。烈於草地中站立良久，向西南方空氣中輕聲道：「璟同，你走過這裡麽？你說了什麽？」無人回答，然身後草浪忽起，彷彿有人轉身。
passages.en: After several days' travel the terrain began to change. The road at first resembled ordinary wilderness; then the colour of the grass shifted to white; then voices rose from within the grass — low as the murmuring of sleep. Listening closely, Wu Lie heard human speech, but the order of words was inverted, meanings scattered, like writing on silk left too long in water: the characters still present, the sense already gone. He crouched down and pressed his ear to the grass. He made out one phrase: '... mother ... the pot ... there is still ...' and another: '... don't, don't go ...' and then fragments too broken to decipher. He understood. These were the last words of the dead, scattered across this place, repeating endlessly among the grass-stems — like shards of a bronze mirror that still dimly remember the songs they once reflected. Wu Lie stood for a long time in the white grass, then spoke quietly toward the air to the southwest: 'Jing Tong — did you pass through here? What did you say?' No answer came. Yet the grass behind him surged in a wave, as though someone had turned around.

zh: 白草無邊聲細碎，死人遺語纏草根。
半句慈母呼兒名，一聲新婦喚郎魂。
英雄側耳聽不得，西風過處舊情痕。
璟同，璟同，汝何在？草答以沉默，又答以聲音。
en: Boundless white grass, and within it, fine-grained sounds — the last words of the dead tangled among the roots.
Half a phrase: a loving mother calling her child's name. One cry: a young bride summoning her husband's soul.
The hero presses his ear close, but cannot hold the words; the west wind passes and leaves only the trace of old feeling.
Jing Tong, Jing Tong — where are you now? The grass answers with silence; then answers again with sound.

passages.zh: 又行數日，前方現一城，然此城甚奇——樓宇整齊，街道筆直，門牌號碼分明，然無一扇門開著，無一窗內有燈，卻有人——無數人，魚貫成列，立於各衙署之外，或坐或站，神情漠然，各執一紙牌，上書所求之事：「申請戶籍核發」、「亡人名錄更正申請」、「魂籍遷移手續辦理」。窗口後有吏員，然吏員皆低頭，翻弄文書，永不抬頭。隊列緩緩移動，然每有人近至窗口，吏員便搖頭曰：「此窗非辦此事，請往第七窗。」第七窗之吏員復曰：「此窗今日停辦，明日請早。」然此地無明日。吳烈穿隊列而過，死者皆讓道，以目光相送，目光中無怨怒，只有無邊倦意。
passages.en: Several more days of walking, and ahead a city appeared. But it was a strange city: its buildings were orderly, its streets straight, the number-plaques on every door clearly marked. Yet not one door stood open, not one window showed a lamp. There were people — countless people, filing in queues outside every government office, some sitting, some standing, their expressions blank. Each held a paper tablet inscribed with their petition: 'Application for household registry issuance.' 'Request to correct an error in the register of the deceased.' 'Processing of soul-registry relocation procedures.' Behind the counter-windows sat clerks, but the clerks all kept their heads bowed, sorting through documents, never once looking up. The queues moved slowly forward. But whenever someone drew close enough to reach a window, the clerk would shake his head and say: 'This window does not handle that matter — please proceed to Window Seven.' The clerk at Window Seven would in turn reply: 'This window has suspended service for today; please come early tomorrow.' Yet in this place there was no tomorrow. Wu Lie passed through the queues; the dead made way for him, watching him go with their eyes. In those eyes there was no anger, no resentment — only an exhaustion without end.

redComment.anchor: 此窗非辦此事，請往第七窗
redComment.zh: 此段寫幽都衙署，表面荒誕，實則深刻。死者猶排隊求辦手續，此乃人世執念之延伸，非鬼怪可怖，乃官僚之永恆。批者以為，此城較十八層地獄更令人心寒。
redComment.en: 'This window does not handle that matter' — on its surface absurdist, yet deeply felt. The dead still queue to petition for procedures; this is the continuation of the living world's attachments into death. Not a horror of demons, but the eternal horror of bureaucracy. The commentator finds this city colder to the heart than all eighteen hells.

passages.zh: 城之西南隅，有一山。白晝望之，山勢巍峨，巖壁陡峭，似可攀登，然近之則覺有異：山石紋路過於規整，苔蘚分佈如以尺量過，陽光打上去，反光之角度微有不對。吳烈手撫石面，冰涼光滑，無一粒沙礫。他繞山一周，至背陰處，見一板壁之縫，手指探入，摸得畫布之質。此山乃畫。不知何人，以極大手筆，繪一座山于此，遠觀無異，近觸則假。吳烈仰頭，對著畫山苦笑：「連山也是假的。璟同若在，必大笑一場。」待至夜，四周一片暗，月出，山形消失，然腳下卻有實實在在的石階，向上延伸，無盡頭。他遂於夜中攀山，每步皆實，但不可回頭看——曾有一次回頭，身後只有虛空，黑暗，以及極遠處某個城市的昏黃燈火。
passages.en: At the southwestern corner of the city stood a mountain. Seen by daylight, it was imposing in scale: sheer cliffs, a commanding silhouette, apparently climbable. Yet drawing close, something felt wrong. The grain of the rock was too regular; the distribution of moss seemed measured by a ruler; sunlight striking the surface reflected back at a slightly incorrect angle. Wu Lie pressed his hand against the stone — cold and smooth, not a single grain of grit. He circled the mountain entirely, and on the shaded side found a crack in a flat surface. He pushed a finger into the crack and felt the texture of painted canvas. The mountain was a painting. Someone, with brush-strokes of enormous scale, had painted a mountain here. Distant, it was indistinguishable from the real. Close, it was false. Wu Lie looked up at the painted mountain and gave a bitter smile. 'Even the mountains here are false. If Jing Tong were here, he would laugh himself hoarse.' When night came and darkness settled over everything, the mountain's silhouette vanished in the darkness — yet beneath Wu Lie's feet there appeared a staircase of entirely solid stone, extending upward without visible end. He climbed the mountain in the dark. Every step was real. But he could not look back. Once, he glanced behind him, and there was only void, only darkness, and in the very far distance the dim yellow glow of a city's lamps.

zh: 晝山是畫夜山真，英雄攀行不回頭。
一步一石皆有據，身後空茫無去路。
璟同笑聲在何處？唯有夜風答山谷。
en: By day the mountain is a painting; by night it is real — the hero climbs and does not look back.
Each step, each stone has its substance; behind him is only void, with no road back.
Where now is the sound of Jing Tong's laughter? Only the night wind answers, returning from the valley.

passages.zh: 山頂之後，地勢驟降，前方有水聲，低沉如嗆咽，非奔流，乃漫淌——忘川也。河面寬闊，水色灰白，若融雪初解而水中攙了塵，不見河底，不見對岸，然有橋，橋上有一老者，背漁蓑，持竹竿，不釣魚，只把竿垂于水中，一動不動，彷彿他本人亦是水中之物，只是尚未沉下去。吳烈近前，老者不動，不看他。吳烈立于橋頭，道：「老丈，此橋可通對岸？」老者曰：「可通。」吳烈道：「敢問可否渡我？」老者曰：「先問你一事。你怕什麼？」吳烈不假思索，答曰：「無所懼。」老者沉默半晌，旋轉過身，第一次看他，目光如積年之水，深而無底，道：「此乃你今日所言最真之語，亦是最不誠之語。渡。」
passages.en: Beyond the mountaintop the land dropped sharply, and ahead Wu Lie heard the sound of water — low, like stifled weeping, not a rushing torrent but a wide, slow spread. The River of Forgetting. The river's surface was broad and grey-white, like meltwater from the first thaw mixed with the dust of years. No bottom was visible, no opposite bank — yet there was a bridge, and on the bridge an old man, wearing a rain-cape of straw, holding a bamboo pole. He was not fishing; he simply let the pole trail in the water and sat motionless, as though he himself were also a thing of the water, only not yet sunk beneath it. Wu Lie approached. The old man did not stir, did not look up. Wu Lie stood at the bridgehead and said: 'Old father — does this bridge cross to the far bank?' The old man said: 'It does.' Wu Lie said: 'May I ask whether you will ferry me across?' The old man said: 'First I ask you one thing. What do you fear?' Without hesitation Wu Lie answered: 'Nothing.' The old man was silent for a long moment. Then he turned, and for the first time he looked at Wu Lie directly. His gaze was like water accumulated over years — deep and without bottom. He said: 'This is the truest thing you have said today, and also the most dishonest. Cross.'

redComment.anchor: 最真之語，亦是最不誠之語
redComment.zh: 河神此語，看似矛盾，實乃洞見。吳烈言「無所懼」，其果無懼乎？正因懼得太深，反言無懼。老子曰「知常曰明」，河神知其常，故明之。批者以為此一問答乃全章之樞紐。
redComment.en: The River-God's reply seems contradictory but is in fact insight. Wu Lie says 'I fear nothing' — does he truly fear nothing? It is precisely because his fear runs so deep that he names its absence. Laozi wrote: 'knowing the constant is called clarity.' The River-God knows his constant, and so sees clearly. The commentator holds this exchange to be the pivot of the entire chapter.

zh: 忘川水深無底色，老者垂竿不問魚。
英雄立橋言無懼，河神一笑水紋起。
真語假語同一言，此岸彼岸同此日。
渡者須知渡非渡，過水之人已非昨日吾。
en: The River of Forgetting, deep and colourless to its bed; the old man trails his pole and does not seek fish.
The hero stands on the bridge and declares he has no fear; the River-God smiles once, and the water's surface stirs.
True speech and false speech are a single utterance; this bank and that bank share the same unmoving day.
Whoever would cross must know that crossing is not crossing — the man who has passed through water is no longer yesterday's self.

passages.zh: 過橋，地色轉深，前方隱現城廓，非大城，乃省城氣象——矮牆灰瓦，牌坊幾座，街燈昏黃，光圈之內皆是浮塵。此即幽都，天下亡魂之暫居之所也。入城之初，有吏執冊核查，見吳烈，翻冊半晌，搖頭曰：「無此人名錄。」烈曰：「吾非死者，乃生人。」吏員瞪目，又翻冊，道：「生人無此例，然亦無明文禁止。暫予登記，列為『未定類』，請勿製造糾紛，勿滯留逾期。」烈領牌入城，牌上寫：「訪客（存疑）。」城中死者見之，多不理睬，少數側目，更少數者低聲相告：「生人。」如報一異鳥入林，無驚，只有遠遠的好奇。
passages.en: After crossing, the colour of the ground deepened, and ahead the outline of a city gradually appeared — not a great capital but a provincial city in character: low walls, grey roof-tiles, a few memorial archways, street-lamps burning dim yellow, their circles of light filled with drifting dust. This was the Dark Metropolis — the temporary dwelling-place of all the departed souls under heaven. At the city's entrance, a clerk with a register checked arrivals. He saw Wu Lie, turned through the register for a long moment, then shook his head: 'No entry for this name.' Wu Lie said: 'I am not among the dead. I am a living person.' The clerk stared, turned through the register again, and said: 'There is no precedent for a living person, but there is no written prohibition either. You will be registered provisionally, categorized as Undetermined Class. Please do not cause disturbances. Do not overstay.' Wu Lie was issued a tablet and entered the city. The tablet read: Visitor (Status Uncertain). The dead inside the city mostly paid him no attention. A few glanced sideways. Fewer still murmured quietly to one another: 'A living person.' As one might report an unusual bird entering a forest — no alarm, only a distant, mild curiosity.

passages.zh: 吳烈於幽都中尋璟同，問於各處。然幽都之大，遠超人想——街道縱橫，巷弄深曲，每一巷皆有數百戶，每一戶皆有其亡者，各自沉默，各坐門前，如在等一封永不投到的信。有老嫗抬頭，問曰：「汝尋何人？」烈曰：「友人，名璟同，壯年，體形高大，笑聲如雷。」嫗搖頭曰：「此處無此人，此人笑聲太響，幽都不留響聲之人——響聲之人，皆送往更深之檔案。」烈問：「何謂更深之檔案？」嫗默然，以手向城之更深處一指，旋低頭，不再理他。
passages.en: Wu Lie moved through the Dark Metropolis searching for Jing Tong, making inquiries at every turning. But the Dark Metropolis was vaster than any living person could conceive: streets crossing in every direction, lanes twisting deep into shadow, each lane holding hundreds of households, each household its own dead person sitting in their own silence before their own door — as though waiting for a letter that would never be delivered. An old woman raised her head and asked: 'Whom are you looking for?' Wu Lie said: 'A friend — name of Jing Tong, a man in his prime, large in frame, with a laugh like thunder.' The old woman shook her head. 'There is no such person here. His laugh was too loud. The Dark Metropolis does not keep those who carry loud sounds — people who carry loud sounds are all sent to the deeper archive.' Wu Lie asked: 'What is the deeper archive?' The old woman was silent. She raised one hand and pointed toward the city's further interior, then lowered her head and paid him no more attention.

passages.zh: 吳烈又行，至城中一廣場，廣場正中有一石碑，高丈餘，碑面密密麻麻刻滿名字——皆亡者之名，字跡工整，然排列無序，非依生卒，非依姓氏，唯依一種無法解讀的邏輯羅列，如一部無目錄之書。烈於碑前細尋，尋至日落（雖此處之日落只是燈光稍稍再暗一些），未見璟同之名。忽見碑角有一行小字，刻曰：「已移往前檔者，不列此碑。」旁有一更小注：「前檔即檔中之無盡處。」吳烈默讀此語，閉目，在碑前向空氣中說：「璟同，你已走到我追不上的地方了。但我還是來了。你能聽見麽？」無聲。然廣場上數片枯葉，忽無風而動。
passages.en: Wu Lie walked on until he reached a wide square at the city's centre. In the middle of the square stood a stone stele, over ten feet high, its surface covered in densely carved names — names of the departed, the calligraphy neat and regular, but arranged in no discernible order: not by date of birth or death, not by surname, only by some logic that could not be read, like a book without a table of contents. Wu Lie searched the stele face carefully, searching until what passed for sunset in this place — which meant only that the lamps dimmed slightly further. He found no name of Jing Tong. Then at the stele's corner he noticed one line of smaller characters carved there: 'Those transferred to the Forward Archive are not listed on this stele.' Beside it, in an even smaller annotation: 'The Forward Archive is the place within the archive that has no end.' Wu Lie read these words in silence. He closed his eyes. Standing before the stele, he spoke into the air: 'Jing Tong — you have gone somewhere I cannot follow. And yet I came anyway. Can you hear me?' Silence. Yet on the square's stone pavement, several dry leaves moved without wind.

zh: 幽都賦
維幽都之在西，地廣而氣昏兮，
街衢整肅，牌匾列兮，燈火如故而無溫兮。
亡者就列，手執牒兮，案無決斷，隊無盡兮。
吏員低首，印不落兮，申請年年，歲月空兮。
生者偶入，目皆側兮，訪客存疑，牌繫頸兮。
璟同不在此城中，笑響已移深檔閣兮。
吳烈立碑，問虛空兮，葉動無風，或有聲兮。
嗚呼，幽都之法度嚴而其情淡，
其序整而其意冷，其名眾而其人失——
非地獄之恐怖，乃省府之永晝兮。
en: Rhapsody of the Dark Metropolis
The Dark Metropolis lies to the west; the land is wide and the air dim.
Its avenues are orderly, its plaques hang in rows; its lamps burn as always, but give no warmth.
The dead form their queues, each holding a document; no case is decided, no queue reaches its end.
The clerks keep their heads bowed; the seal does not descend; petitions accumulate year on year; time passes empty.
When a living person enters, every eye turns sideways; tagged as Visitor, Status Uncertain, the tablet hangs at the neck.
Jing Tong is not in this city — his laugh was too loud; it has been moved to the deeper archive.
Wu Lie stands before the stele and questions the void; leaves stir without wind, as though something, perhaps, makes sound.
Alas — the Dark Metropolis is strict in its ordinances and thin in feeling;
ordered in its arrangements yet cold in its intent; its roster of names is vast, yet its persons are lost —
not the terror of hell, but the permanent noon of a provincial office.

passages.zh: 是夜，吳烈宿於城中一客舍——幽都亦有客舍，供偶入之生人或未定類者暫住，被褥灰薄，枕頭重如石，窗外有隊伍徹夜排列，腳步聲無聲，然隱約可感地板之震顫。烈不能眠，坐於燈下，取懷中帛書，以炭筆寫字，非給雲素華之記錄，乃給璟同之書信，無收信人，無署名，寫道：「吾至幽都，未見汝，聞汝已在更深之處。吾不知何謂更深，然明日將繼續向西。汝若尚有知覺，勿等吾，先走。吾只是要告訴汝：桃林之誓，吾仍記。」寫畢，折好，置於燈焰旁，頃刻成灰。灰升，散於屋內，無去向。
passages.en: That night Wu Lie lodged at an inn within the city — for the Dark Metropolis had inns, built to accommodate the occasional living visitor or Undetermined guest. The bedding was thin and grey; the pillow was heavy as stone. Outside the window, queues continued to form through the night: their footsteps made no sound, yet the faint trembling of the floorboards beneath could be felt. Wu Lie could not sleep. He sat beneath the lamp, drew the silk-cloth from his chest, and wrote with a charcoal brush. Not the record he kept for Yun Suhua — this was a letter to Jing Tong. No recipient, no sender. He wrote: 'I have reached the Dark Metropolis. I did not find you. I am told you have gone somewhere deeper. I do not know what deeper means, but tomorrow I will continue westward. If you still have awareness of any kind, do not wait for me. Go ahead. I only needed to tell you: the oath in the peach grove — I still hold it.' When he finished, he folded the letter carefully and placed it beside the lamp's flame. In a moment it was ash. The ash rose, dispersed through the room, and had nowhere to go.

redComment.zh: 此語回應第一回桃林結義，首尾呼應，情深一至於此。信燒於幽都，灰散無歸，此寫法與古人焚紙以祭之俗暗合，然烈所燒者非祭品，乃心聲——比祭品更重，亦更難收。
redComment.en: This line echoes the oath sworn in the peach grove of Chapter 1 — head and tail answering each other across the full span. A letter burned in the Dark Metropolis, its ash dispersing with nowhere to go: the method secretly accords with the old custom of burning paper offerings for the dead. But what Wu Lie burns is not an offering — it is the voice of his heart. Heavier than any offering, and harder to retrieve.

zh: 燒一紙書與璟同，灰飛無聲落幽都。
桃林血誓猶在口，幽都無人能作主。
生者書信死者讀，不知冥路幾多途。
en: He burns a paper letter for Jing Tong; the ash falls without sound in the Dark Metropolis.
The blood-oath of the peach grove still lives in his mouth, yet in the Dark Metropolis no one holds authority over such things.
A living man's letter, read by one who is dead — and no one knows how many roads the dark passage holds.

passages.zh: 天將明未明之際，吳烈起身，出客舍，穿城而過，向城之更西處行去。城門守吏核查牌符，見「訪客（存疑）」，翻冊，道：「逾期一夜，罰錄一筆，請簽字。」烈簽字，吏員在冊上添一行小字，無人知是何字。出城之時，烈忽轉身，望幽都昏黃之燈火，低聲道：「無言君，我知此城是你的轄境。」此名，乃西極之女神，主宰更深冥界者——他未見她，然城中一呼一吸皆似含她之意志。說完，他轉身西行，幽都之燈在身後漸漸淡去，如一場尚未說完便終止的夢，或一段話，說到一半，發聲者忽然閉口，而聽者仍立在原處，等待那未曾到來的下半句。
passages.en: When the sky was poised between dark and light, Wu Lie rose, left the inn, passed through the city, and walked westward toward the city's further boundary. The gate-guard checked his tablet: seeing 'Visitor, Status Uncertain,' he turned through the register and said: 'You have overstayed by one night. A demerit notation will be added. Please sign.' Wu Lie signed. The clerk added a line of small characters to the register; no one could tell what the characters said. As he stepped through the gate, Wu Lie turned once and looked back at the dim yellow lamps of the Dark Metropolis. He said, quietly: 'Lady Silence — I know this city is within your jurisdiction.' This name: the goddess of the western extreme, sovereign of the deeper reaches of the dark — he had not seen her, yet every breath the city drew seemed to carry something of her will. He finished speaking, turned, and walked west. The lamps of the Dark Metropolis faded behind him, gradually, like a dream that ends before it has finished speaking, or like a sentence, broken off at its midpoint, the speaker having suddenly gone silent while the listener still stands there, waiting for the second half that never comes.
